source.note: Seed batch generated from 3 LeanScale Podcast transcripts during initial system bring-up. Use as the reference example for what a real batch looks like.

transcripts.topic: Operations-background CRO, AI-native services in heavy industries
Enterprise AI selling, MEDDPICC, decision criteria as a sales weapon

topics.title: The GTM Engineer Can't Replace Your RevOps Leader
topics.angle: The market is selling executives a fantasy that AI plus GTM engineers replaces the RevOps team. The layer that breaks is the translation of business strategy to tactical execution.
topics.sourceQuote: I remember in that same conversation having that conversation with a GoToMarket engineer, at one point he asked me what ARR was.

topics.title: Why, What, How: The Three Layers of GTM Work
topics.angle: GTM engineering is built for the How. CEOs and CROs own the Why. The VP of RevOps owns the What — that translation layer is where most AI-first orgs are about to discover they're missing critical infrastructure.
topics.sourceQuote: The go-to-market engineering layer is extremely capable of handling the how... I think that VP of RevOps role though translates all that into the what?

topics.title: AI Won't Cut Your Headcount Until You Fix Your Data
topics.angle: Boards mandating RevOps headcount cuts because AI are skipping a step. You can't layer agents on bad data architecture and inconsistent processes. The investment to reduce headcount has to happen BEFORE the headcount reduction.
topics.sourceQuote: In order for there to be a reduction in headcount, there has to be rigorous operations, a lot of standardization, and incredible data architecture in order to get there. It can't just replace people.

topics.title: Run the Business vs. Transform the Business: The Impossible Mandate
topics.angle: RevOps leaders are being asked to AI-transform the GTM org with the same headcount that's already drowning in forecasting, QBRs, comp, and territory planning. The math doesn't work.
topics.sourceQuote: How do I run the business while transforming the business? Well, all the priorities are gonna stay the same.

topics.title: The Eisenhower Matrix for AI-Enabled Operators
topics.angle: AI gives operators 10x output capacity, but the field can only absorb so much change. Doing 3 things that move the needle still beats doing 10 mediocre things.
topics.sourceQuote: Just because you can move so much faster, delivering three things really meaningfully... is still more important than delivering ten things that may or may not be adopted.

topics.title: Start Analog, Then Layer AI
topics.angle: The highest-quality AI workflow starts with you writing your hypothesis manually. AI then finds examples, crunches data, pressure-tests. Skip the analog step and the work collapses under the first probing question.
topics.sourceQuote: Start in analog mode, write your thoughts out manually... You can tell when somebody puts together a presentation that they didn't really do. They just AI slopped it together.

topics.title: Open a Google Sheet: The 4-Hour Exercise That Separates AI Operators from AI Tourists
topics.angle: If you're a senior analyst waiting for your company to enable you on AI, you've already lost. Open a Google Sheet, list recurring tasks, mark level of effort, ask what an agent could do. 4 hours puts you ahead of 90% of peers.
topics.sourceQuote: I would open up a Google Sheet and I would write down what are the core things that I do for my job that are repeating.

topics.title: An Operator Is Only 100% Honest in the Exit Interview
topics.angle: Three reasons companies hire consultants: bandwidth, expertise, and political cover. Sometimes the org needs someone outside the promotion chain to say the truth.
topics.sourceQuote: Really as an operator, the only time you get to be truly a hundred percent honest is in your exit interview.

topics.title: Do the Next Job Before You Get It
topics.angle: Promotion never comes if you're already not operating at the next level. AI is making this gap exponentially wider — top performers compound, everyone else stalls.
topics.sourceQuote: I'm gonna do the next job that I want before I get it.

topics.title: Operators Make Better Sellers Than Sellers Do
topics.angle: In operationally-intensive industries, classically-trained SaaS sellers get the demo through the door but lose the deal. I can teach an operator to sell. I can't teach a seller five years of industry experience.
topics.sourceQuote: Operators who then become sellers, which is how I think about hiring in these industries, actually lean towards people who have done the thing or operated in the space.

topics.title: The First Contract Is the Audition
topics.angle: In heavy industries, expansion isn't a product problem — it's a trust problem. The first deal is the cover charge for being asked bigger questions.
topics.sourceQuote: All the work actually happens after the first deal is closed. It's all about that's the audition is the first part.

topics.title: RevOps Is the Language of Rapid Scalability
topics.angle: RevOps isn't 'is Salesforce clean.' It's the language your company uses to test, measure, learn, and decide. Without it, you're not making decisions — you're guessing.
topics.sourceQuote: RevOps is the language in which companies test, measure, learn, and ultimately drive rapid scalability.

topics.title: Why Tight TAM Industries Punish Bad Delivery
topics.angle: When your industry is a duopoly of 20 logos, one botched implementation doesn't just lose a customer — it closes the entire market. Word of mouth in tight communities can replace your outbound machine. Or annihilate it.
topics.sourceQuote: The word of mouth impact from a referral... can be far more impactful than any outbounding strategy AI enabled or otherwise.

topics.title: AI-Native Services: The Quality Story Nobody Is Telling
topics.angle: Every AI narrative is about cutting headcount. The real story is scaling quality. The biggest bottleneck in services isn't hours — it's maintaining the founding team's standard as you grow.
topics.sourceQuote: The biggest bottleneck is scaling the quality. Typically when it starts, you have somebody really passionate, really skilled... And then it starts to erode as you grow and scale the team.

topics.title: We Got 10x Faster and Almost Lost Revenue
topics.angle: When we automated half our delivery time, our first reaction was panic — were we cannibalizing billable hours? Then customers found more work. The fear curve every services firm is about to ride.
topics.sourceQuote: We're spending half the time and we're not billing anymore for it. So we're actually losing revenue the better we do.

topics.title: The Build Order: CRO, Then RevOps, Then Enablement, Then AEs
topics.angle: Most founders' first GTM hire after themselves is an AE. That's why most early AEs fail. The non-negotiable build order: CRO, then RevOps + Enablement, THEN a single AE walks in.
topics.sourceQuote: When you hire me, there's an expectation you're also hiring somebody in rev ops and somebody in enablement. That's my team.

topics.title: Decision Criteria Is the Sales Weapon Nobody Talks About
topics.angle: The rep who waits for a buyer's decision criteria is a sitting duck. Buyers don't know how to buy AI. Build the editable scorecard. Hand it to them. The seller who shapes decision criteria can't lose.
topics.sourceQuote: The reps that influence decision criteria are the best reps, period.

topics.title: The CHRO Is No Longer the Economic Buyer for HR AI — IT Is
topics.angle: The classic enterprise sale used to terminate at the department head. AI changed that. IT now owns the architecture and can kill deals weeks after HR said yes.
topics.sourceQuote: HR has gone to get the deal done. IT has blocked the deal at the end of the process because they weren't included in the process itself.

topics.title: POCs Are the New Default — and Most Don't Convert
topics.angle: Risk, compliance, and governance kill POC-to-enterprise conversions at the finish line. If you're not absorbing security questionnaires upfront with a trust portal, you have a qualification gap that wrecks your forecast.
topics.sourceQuote: These POCs that are being implemented now tend to actually not convert to full enterprise deals because of risk compliance and governance.

topics.title: Forward Deployed Engineers Are Not a Trend — They're a Requirement
topics.angle: Every serious AI company has them. Selling agentic without an FDE on implementation is a bet against your customer's success. Bake the FDE-to-account ratio into your growth model.
topics.sourceQuote: Forward deployed engineers are in every one of these organizations. We have something similar.

topics.title: Stop Talking About Top of Funnel AI — Mid Funnel Is Where the Gold Is
topics.angle: LinkedIn is saturated with 10,000-step lead workflows. Meanwhile auto-generated SOWs from call transcripts, AI deal coaching tuned to your playbook, and GTM diagnostics that turn 2-week scoping into a 1-hour deliverable are eating the revenue ceiling.
topics.sourceQuote: What is really under discussed is how to leverage AI for mid funnel.

topics.title: Auto-Populate MEDDIC from Gong — Then Compare It to Rep Input
topics.angle: Reps inflate MEDDIC fields. The unlock isn't replacing rep input — it's auto-populating from Gong transcripts and running a compare-and-contrast. Triangulation surfaces bad reps, bad deals, and process gaps at once.
topics.sourceQuote: I came to our revops guy and I said, hey, is there a way for us to be able to auto-populate medic from Gong transcripts? And sure enough we actually got it syncing.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Auto-Populate MEDDIC from Gong — Then Compare It to Rep Input
posts.postText:
Reps inflate MEDDIC. That's not a rep problem, it's a data architecture problem.

Here's a workflow we shipped for a client this month:

- Gong transcripts feed an LLM that extracts MEDDIC fields
- The output writes to a parallel set of properties in HubSpot (AI_Metrics, AI_Champion, etc.)
- Reps continue to fill in the standard MEDDIC fields as normal
- A nightly job runs a compare-and-contrast and flags deltas above a confidence threshold

The magic isn't replacing the rep input. It's the triangulation.

When the AI says no economic buyer was identified on the last three calls and the rep has the EB filled in green, that's not a forecasting problem to solve in QBR. That's a coaching moment for next Tuesday.

We found three patterns in the first 30 days:

1. Bad reps over-index on optimism in fields they can't defend
2. Good reps actually under-fill, then the AI surfaces signal they forgot to log
3. The whole exercise exposes which MEDDIC fields your playbook never actually trained reps on

DM me if you want the prompt structure. It's not complicated — the win is in the comparison layer, not the extraction.
posts.voiceNotes: Tactical playbook in Jake's lane — concrete workflow with named tools, short bullets, soft DM CTA. Opens with a result statement, not a contrarian hook.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Forward Deployed Engineers Are Not a Trend — They're a Requirement
posts.postText:
Spent last week reviewing implementation models from six AI vendors a customer was evaluating.

Every serious one has forward deployed engineers on the implementation team. The ones that don't are quietly losing their POCs to production.

A few patterns I'm seeing:

- The FDE is not a CSM with a technical title. They write code, they own data model decisions, they sit in the customer's Slack
- The gearing ratio matters more than the headcount. One FDE per 5-7 implementations is the sustainable ratio I keep seeing
- Vendors who outsource implementation to SI partners are seeing 30-40% POC-to-production conversion. Vendors with in-house FDEs are seeing double that

This is the part of the AI services story that doesn't get airtime on LinkedIn.

At LeanScale Labs we built our delivery model around this same shape — the senior partner runs the engagement, an engineer sits inside the customer's stack, and the workflow layer is owned by someone who can actually ship.

If you're evaluating agentic platforms right now, ask the vendor about their FDE-to-account ratio. The answer tells you whether your implementation will live or die.
posts.voiceNotes: Pattern recognition voice. Opens with the artifact. Drops LeanScale Labs as reference, doesn't pitch.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: We Got 10x Faster and Almost Lost Revenue
posts.postText:
We rebuilt a client's lead routing in 3 days last month. Used to be a 3-week engagement.

First reaction internally was panic. We're billing a fraction of what we used to and the quality is higher. Were we about to cannibalize our own revenue line?

Here's what actually happened:

The customer saw the output, asked us to re-architect their MQL-to-SQL handoff next, then their territory model, then their forecast accuracy workflow. The work we shipped on day 3 unlocked four engagements we didn't know existed.

The lesson I keep coming back to: speed isn't the threat to a services firm. Stagnation in scope is.

When you can deliver in 3 days what used to take 3 weeks, you find out very quickly whether your customer actually has more problems to solve. Spoiler — they always do. The bottleneck was never their willingness to engage. It was our throughput.

This is the part of the AI services pivot most firms aren't ready for. The fear curve is real for about 60 days, then it inverts.

Shoutout to the team that pulled the rebuild off — Anthony, Cam, and the engineering crew at LeanScale Labs.
posts.voiceNotes: Case study with concrete outcome. Personal story register but Jake's version — no vulnerability bait. Team shoutout close.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Why, What, How: The Three Layers of GTM Work
posts.postText:
There's a confusion I keep running into in conversations with founders about GTM engineering.

It usually goes: we have an AI engineer, we don't need a RevOps leader anymore.

The three layers of GTM work explain why this almost never works.

The Why is owned by the CEO and CRO. What market are we attacking, what does the motion look like, what does winning mean this year.

The What is owned by the VP of RevOps. What are the processes that execute the strategy. What's the lead lifecycle, the territory model, the comp plan, the forecasting cadence. This is the translation layer.

The How is owned by GTM engineering. What systems, what integrations, what agents, what automations make the What actually run at scale.

A GTM engineer can absolutely automate the How. What they cannot do is decide what the What should be. That requires someone who's run a sales floor, watched a comp plan break, sat through a board meeting where forecast accuracy was the whole conversation.

If you skip the What, the How becomes a beautifully engineered system that solves the wrong problem.

Most AI-first orgs are about to learn this the hard way.
posts.voiceNotes: Soft educational explainer. Jake's earnest teaching voice. No hook gymnastics.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: The Eisenhower Matrix for AI-Enabled Operators
posts.postText:
AI gave my team 10x output capacity this year. Then we made the mistake of actually trying to use all of it.

The field can only absorb so much change. Shipping 10 things that don't get adopted is worse than shipping 3 things that move the needle.

Built this prioritization matrix for our team at LeanScale Labs. Swipe through.

The filter isn't 'what can I do faster.' It's level of effort, urgency, and importance — same matrix Eisenhower would recognize, just with an AI capacity multiplier on the LOE axis.

Happy to share the Notion template if it's useful. DM me.
posts.voiceNotes: Carousel caption. Opens with personal observation but in pragmatic register. Soft DM CTA.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 5-slide carousel. Slide 1: Title card — 'The AI-Enabled Operator Matrix' in Plus Jakarta Sans, dark mode, lime accent. Slide 2: Classic 2x2 Eisenhower matrix (Urgent/Not Urgent x Important/Not Important) with the four quadrants labeled (Do Now, Schedule, Delegate, Drop). Slide 3: The same matrix overlaid with an LOE color gradient — lime for low-effort with AI, purple for high-effort even with AI. Slide 4: Worked example — 4 real GTM tasks plotted on the matrix (forecast review, territory rebuild, weekly enablement email, ad-hoc rep coaching ask). Slide 5: 'Three things that ship and stick beat ten things that ship and don't.' with a 'DM for the template' CTA. LeanScale branding throughout — dark background (#1A1A1A), lime/purple accents, www.leanscale.team footer.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Stop Talking About Top of Funnel AI — Mid Funnel Is Where the Gold Is
posts.postText:
Everyone is shipping top-of-funnel AI workflows. Lead enrichment, sequencer agents, 10-step Clay tables.

Meanwhile the mid-funnel — where revenue actually gets made — is wide open.

Here's what we build with daily at LeanScale to compress the middle:

𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼-𝗴𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗦𝗢𝗪𝘀 — pulled straight from Fireflies call transcripts, turned around in minutes instead of days.

𝗔𝗜 𝗱𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗰𝗼𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 — tuned to the customer's MEDDPICC and playbook, not generic Gong scorecards.

𝗚𝗧𝗠 𝗱𝗶𝗮𝗴𝗻𝗼𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗰𝘀 — 2-week scoping exercises collapsed into a 1-hour deliverable.

𝗤𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝘀𝘆𝗻𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘀𝗶𝘀 — discovery notes auto-mapped to next-step plays.

Great reps will still close. But the ones leveraging mid-funnel AI are forecasting cleaner and ramping quicker.

More on the stack we use for this in the coming weeks. 🫡

#revops #gtm
posts.voiceNotes: Unicode bold for tool names, em-dash one-line descriptions, salute emoji close, hashtag bundle.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Auto-Populate MEDDIC from Gong — Then Compare It to Rep Input
posts.postText:
Reps inflate MEDDPICC fields. Always have, always will.

The unlock isn't replacing rep input — it's triangulating it.

Here's the workflow we've helped a handful of B2B startups stand up:

𝟭. 𝗦𝘆𝗻𝗰 𝗚𝗼𝗻𝗴 (𝗼𝗿 𝗙𝗶𝗿𝗲𝗳𝗹𝗶𝗲𝘀) 𝘁𝗼 𝗛𝘂𝗯𝗦𝗽𝗼𝘁/𝗦𝗮𝗹𝗲𝘀𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗰𝗲 — auto-extract Metrics, Economic Buyer, Decision Criteria, Pain straight from transcript.

𝟮. 𝗞𝗲𝗲𝗽 𝗿𝗲𝗽 𝗶𝗻𝗽𝘂𝘁 𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘃𝗲 — don't kill the manual fields. You want both sources.

𝟯. 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝗮 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗿𝗲-𝗮𝗻𝗱-𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘀𝘁 𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄 — surface the delta between what the rep entered and what the call actually said.

𝟰. 𝗥𝗲𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄 𝘄𝗲𝗲𝗸𝗹𝘆 𝗶𝗻 𝗽𝗶𝗽𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗹 — gaps surface bad deals, bad reps, and gaps in the sales process all at once.

The forecast gets cleaner. Coaching gets specific. And nobody can hide a Closed Lost behind an inflated qualification score.

We wrote up the full implementation pattern in our docs — link in comments. 🫡

#revops #meddpicc
posts.voiceNotes: Numbered steps with Unicode-bold headers, 'we've helped' framing, named tools, soft CTA, salute close.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: We Got 10x Faster and Almost Lost Revenue
posts.postText:
We recently rebuilt a chunk of our GTM diagnostic delivery using AI workflows.

What used to take a senior consultant two weeks now ships in about two hours.

First reaction internally was honestly a little panicked — were we cannibalizing our own billable hours?

Here's what actually happened:

Customer #1 saw the turnaround, asked us to scope two more workstreams that week.

Customer #2 used the time we saved to expand into a full RevOps retainer.

Customer #3 referred us to their portfolio sister company before we'd even invoiced.

The lesson for any services firm watching the AI shift: speed doesn't shrink the pie. It expands what the customer trusts you with.

Passing the value back is the moat. Hoarding the efficiency is the trap.

More on how we're rebuilding LeanScale delivery for the AI-native era coming soon. 🫡

#revops #servicesassoftware
posts.voiceNotes: Opens with 'We recently', confident operator tone, numbered customer outcomes, salute close.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Forward Deployed Engineers Are Not a Trend — They're a Requirement
posts.postText:
Pattern we're seeing across the AI-native B2B startups we work with:

Every serious one has a Forward Deployed Engineer function. Or something that looks an awful lot like one.

The ones that don't are watching POCs die in production.

Why it matters for GTM:

Agentic products carry orders of magnitude more customer complexity at implementation than a typical SaaS rollout. Sales gets the deal across the line. The FDE keeps it from unraveling in week 3.

If you're modeling growth for an agentic product, the gearing ratio of FDEs to accounts belongs in the model alongside AE quota capacity. Treat it as a CAC line, not a post-sale afterthought.

The AI-native services firms (us included) are building this muscle for our customers right now — pairing technical implementation horsepower with our GTM strategists on every engagement.

Forward deployed isn't a hire. It's a stage of the customer journey. 🫡

#revops #gtm
posts.voiceNotes: Pattern recognition opener, 'we' framing, sharp POV without contrarian bait, salute close.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: The Build Order: CRO, Then RevOps, Then Enablement, Then AEs
posts.postText:
Most founders we talk to want to hire their first AE before they've hired their CRO.

It's the #1 reason early AEs fail.

We put together a simple visual on the non-negotiable build order for a B2B GTM org — and where the wheels come off when you skip a step.

Swipe through to see:

The sequence we've seen work across 50+ hyper-growth startups (Mistral AI, Clio, Chainguard, and others)

The two roles that have to be in seat BEFORE the first AE

What ramp times look like when AEs hit a built machine vs. chaos

Where fractional RevOps fits in the early stages (and where it doesn't)

Full breakdown is also up on docs.leanscale.team for anyone who'd rather read than swipe. 🫡

#revops #gtm #startups
posts.voiceNotes: Caption pointing to visual, customer name-drops, docs reference, salute close.

Slide 3: Side-by-side comparison: 'AEs into a built machine' (ramp: 30-60 days) vs. 'AEs into chaos' (ramp: 6+ months or never). posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: RevOps Is the Language of Rapid Scalability
posts.postText:
RevOps isn't 'is Salesforce clean?'

It's the language your company uses to test, measure, learn, and decide.

Think about it. Every decision a GTM team makes — where to invest reps, how to price, what to enable on, where to expand — only works if you have a shared way to measure and talk about it.

That's RevOps.

Without that language in place, sales-first leaders end up jumping straight to enablement, comp tweaks, and pricing experiments. All worthwhile. But you can't tell if any of it actually worked.

From Sales and Marketing to Customer Success and Finance — RevOps is multiple departments playing in harmony. The conductor is the one making sure everyone is reading the same sheet music.

If you're hiring your first RevOps leader, don't ask 'can they run reports?'

Ask 'can they build the language the whole company uses to make decisions?'

That's the unlock.
posts.voiceNotes: Fragment-as-correction opener, 'playing in harmony' metaphor, calm and teacherly.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: The Build Order: CRO, Then RevOps, Then Enablement, Then AEs
posts.postText:
Most founders' first GTM hire after themselves is an AE.

It's also why most early AEs fail.

Here's the build order I'd defend on any whiteboard:

1. CRO (or fractional sales leader) — owns the strategy and the number
2. RevOps — builds the measurement backbone, comp plans, territory design, and reporting
3. Enablement — codifies the playbook, ramp, and onboarding
4. AEs — drop into a built machine and ramp in weeks instead of quarters

The instinct to hire an AE first is understandable. You need revenue. But an AE walking into a company with no comp plan, no territories, no playbook, and no reporting isn't selling — they're building. And they're not great at building. That's not what you hired them for.

When the foundation is there, a good AE ramps in 60-90 days.

When it isn't, that same AE ramps in 4 quarters. Or never.

If you're an early-stage founder staring at a $0 RevOps budget and a hiring plan full of AEs, flip the order. It's the single highest-leverage decision you'll make this year.

Happy to chat through what that looks like for your stage — let's connect!
posts.voiceNotes: Numbered list, plain symbols, frames AE-first as 'understandable' not wrong, 'let's connect!' close.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: The GTM Engineer Can't Replace Your RevOps Leader
posts.postText:
Seeing a pattern across conversations the last few months.

Executives are being sold a story that AI plus a GTM engineer replaces the RevOps team.

I get the appeal. One smart technical hire, a stack of tools, agents handling the rest. Clean org chart, smaller budget.

Here's where it breaks.

The GTM engineering layer is incredible at the How — how to wire systems, how to build agents, how to automate workflows. That part is real.

But someone still has to own the What. What's the territory model? What's the capacity plan? What does the comp plan need to reward this year vs. last year? What's the segmentation strategy that survives going from 5 reps to 50?

That translation layer — business strategy to tactical execution — doesn't get built by someone who's never run a forecast call or sat in a QBR.

The orgs winning this transition aren't choosing between a RevOps leader and a GTM engineer.

They're hiring both, and being clear about what each one owns.

If you're rebuilding your GTM ops org right now, that's the conversation to have before the headcount conversation.
posts.voiceNotes: Pattern recognition opener, 'I get the appeal' softens contrarian edge, forward-looking close not hard sell.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: The Eisenhower Matrix for AI-Enabled Operators
posts.postText:
AI gives operators 10x output capacity.

But the field can only absorb so much change at once.

Doing 3 things that move the needle still beats doing 10 mediocre things nobody adopts.

Swipe through for the prioritization filter I'm using with our RevOps team right now — Level of Effort, Urgency, and Importance, mapped against what AI can actually accelerate vs. what still needs human judgment.

The goal isn't to do more.

It's to do the right things, faster.
posts.voiceNotes: Short caption, points to visual. 'with our RevOps team right now' adds team-first credibility.

Slide 2: 2x2 matrix — Level of Effort (X-axis, Low to High) x Business Impact (Y-axis, Low to High). Quadrants: Quick Wins (low effort, high impact), Big Bets (high effort, high impact), Fill-In Tasks (low effort, low impact), Avoid (high effort, low impact). Slide 3: Same matrix with AI overlay — lime checkmarks on quadrants AI accelerates well, purple caution on quadrants needing human judgment. Slide 4: 3-step takeaway: '1. List every recurring task. 2. Score effort + impact. 3. Pick the top 3 — not the top 10.' Branded in LeanScale dark mode with lime and purple accents.

params.title: The AI Operator's Prioritization Matrix.
params.body: What I'm using with our RevOps team right now.

params.title: Level of Effort × Business Impact.
items: Quick Wins — low effort, high impact
Big Bets — high effort, high impact
Fill-In Tasks — low effort, low impact

params.title: Where AI actually accelerates.
params.body: Lime = AI does this well today. Purple = still needs human judgment. Score every task against both axes before you commit.

params.title: Three-step exercise.
params.body: 1. List every recurring task. 2. Score effort + impact. 3. Pick the top 3 — not the top 10.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Do the Next Job Before You Get It
posts.postText:
Best career advice I ever heard, paraphrased from a friend who's moved up the ranks faster than anyone I know:

'Do the next job before you get it.'

At the time I thought it was a cliche. Then I started watching the people around me who actually got promoted, and the ones who didn't.

The pattern was identical every time.

The folks who moved up were already operating one level above their title. Sitting in on conversations they technically didn't need to be in. Pulling in cross-functional partners to fix things nobody asked them to fix. Writing the comp plan recommendation before the comp plan question got asked.

The folks who didn't move up were doing exactly what was in their job description. And doing it well. That was the whole problem.

What AI is doing right now is making this gap exponentially wider. The operators who are leaning in, opening Google Sheets, experimenting with agents, drafting workflows nobody asked for — they're compounding. The ones waiting for their company to enable them are watching the gap grow in real time.

If you're early in your career and want to move fast, the answer hasn't changed.

Figure out what your manager is doing. Then start doing pieces of it.

The promotion is the lagging indicator, not the leading one.
posts.voiceNotes: Credits a friend rather than self-aggrandizing. Connects timeless advice to AI moment. Sincere teacherly close.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: AI Won't Cut Your Headcount Until You Fix Your Data
posts.postText:
Boards are mandating RevOps headcount cuts because AI is supposed to absorb the work.

They're skipping a step.

You can't layer agents on bad data architecture and inconsistent processes — the agents just fail faster and louder.

The uncomfortable math: the investment to reduce headcount has to happen BEFORE the headcount reduction. Standardize the processes. Rebuild the data model. Get the definitions agreed-upon and verifiable. Then — and only then — does the agent layer actually compound.

Most leadership teams refuse to fund that arc. They want the cost-out story without the capex story.

After 3-5 GTM leader conversations a week, the pattern is clear:

→ Teams that funded the data and process work first are running 30-40% leaner today.
→ Teams that cut first and tried to backfill with agents are quietly rehiring under different titles.

AI is predictably good when the work is structured, and predictably bad when it's not.

Fix the inputs. The outputs follow.
posts.voiceNotes: Board-mandate observation opener, 'skipping a step' reframe, 'predictably good/bad' verbatim, 3-5 leaders/week cadence reference.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Auto-Populate MEDDIC from Gong — Then Compare It to Rep Input
posts.postText:
Reps inflate MEDDIC fields. Always have.

The unlock isn't replacing rep input — it's running both in parallel and triangulating.

Here's the workflow we've seen working across the portfolio:

1. Sync Gong transcripts into HubSpot (or Salesforce). Tag each call by deal stage.

2. Run an AI scoring layer against an agreed-upon MEDDIC definition — not a generic prompt. The definition has to be owned by the CRO and operationalized by RevOps.

3. Keep rep-entered MEDDIC fields exactly where they are. Don't replace them.

4. Build a compare-and-contrast view: rep input vs. AI-from-transcript input, side by side, on every deal over a certain ACV.

5. Surface the deltas in weekly forecast reviews.

What you'll find in 30 days:

→ The reps inflating champion strength on every deal.
→ The deals with no economic buyer that have been forecast for two quarters.
→ The gaps in your sales process itself — places where MEDDIC just can't get filled in because the motion doesn't support it.

AI scores deals predictably well when the definitions are structured. Get the definitions right first. Then orchestrate the rest.
posts.voiceNotes: Numbered steps, 'agreed-upon definition' and 'orchestrate' vocabulary, parallel bullet structure, clean declarative close.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: The CHRO Is No Longer the Economic Buyer for HR AI — IT Is
posts.postText:
The classic enterprise sale used to terminate at the department head.

AI broke that.

We've watched it happen on three deals in the last 60 days: HR signs off, procurement signs off, the rep is forecasting commit — and then IT kills the deal at the finish line because they were never looped in.

The pattern is the same every time:

→ IT now owns the AI architecture and transformation strategy of the business.
→ Anything that touches employee data, model training, or workflow automation routes through them.
→ They have veto power even when the budget sits in another department.

If you're still mapping accounts the 2019 way — one economic buyer, one champion, one set of decision criteria — you're forecasting deals you'll never close.

The fix isn't more discovery. It's a multi-threaded motion that treats IT as a co-buyer from the first qualification call, not a stakeholder you brief at contract review.

This isn't just an HR-tech problem. It's playing out in finance, legal, ops, and marketing AI deals too.

Map the AI buying committee like it's 2026, because it is.
posts.voiceNotes: 'Three deals in 60 days' pattern anchor, arrow bullets, calm authority, declarative landing.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: The Build Order: CRO, Then RevOps, Then Enablement, Then AEs
posts.postText:
Most founders' first GTM hire after themselves is an AE.

It's also why most early AEs fail.

The non-negotiable B2B SaaS build order — the one we see work, repeatedly, across the portfolio:

1. CRO (or fractional equivalent)
2. RevOps
3. Enablement
4. AEs

Skip any phase and the AE walks into chaos. Hit the order, and they ramp in weeks instead of quarters.

Swipe through for the phase-by-phase breakdown — what each hire owns, what they need in place before they start, and the most common failure mode at each step.

More on this in The LeanScale Letter.
posts.voiceNotes: Short caption, 'build order/phases/portfolio' vocabulary, Letter CTA, frames AE-first as reframe not dunk.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 4-slide carousel. Slide 1: Title card 'The B2B SaaS GTM Build Order' on dark-mode background (#1A1A1A), lime green accent, Plus Jakarta Sans. Slide 2: Phase 1 — CRO. Owns: revenue strategy, hiring plan, board narrative. Needs in place: founder-led sales repeatable, ~$1-3M ARR. Failure mode: hiring a CRO too early as a glorified AE. Slide 3: Phase 2 — RevOps + Phase 3 — Enablement (side by side). RevOps owns: data model, forecast, comp, territory. Enablement owns: onboarding, playbooks, certification. Failure mode: hiring AEs before either exists. Slide 4: Phase 4 — AEs. Owns: pipeline + close. Needs in place: ICP defined, CRM clean, playbook documented, ramp plan written. Failure mode: hiring 5 AEs in one quarter before the system exists. Each slide uses lime-green numbered headers, off-white body text, small LeanScale wordmark in corner.

params.title: The B2B SaaS GTM Build Order.
params.body: The sequence we see work, repeatedly, across the portfolio.
Owns revenue strategy, hiring plan, board narrative. Needs founder-led sales repeatable and ~$1-3M ARR before the seat is real.

params.title: RevOps + Enablement run in parallel.
items: RevOps owns the data, forecast, comp, territory
Failure mode: hiring AEs before either of these exists

params.body: Needs in place: ICP defined, CRM clean, playbook documented, ramp plan written. Skip the prep and a $250k seller becomes a $50k contributor.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Stop Talking About Top of Funnel AI — Mid Funnel Is Where the Gold Is
posts.postText:
LinkedIn is saturated with 10,000-step Clay workflows for top-of-funnel.

Meanwhile, the real revenue ceiling is being eaten in the mid-funnel — and almost no one is talking about it.

The problem has nothing to do with the technology. It has everything to do with where teams are pointing it.

What we're actually seeing move the needle in mid-funnel:

→ Auto-generated SOWs from discovery call transcripts. A two-week scoping exercise turns into a one-hour deliverable.

→ AI deal coaching tuned to the company's actual playbook, not a generic methodology. The coaching gets specific because the inputs are specific.

→ GTM diagnostics that turn a multi-week assessment into a single-call output — usable in the same week the prospect raised the question.

→ Mid-funnel scoring against agreed-upon qualification criteria, so the deals being worked are the deals worth working.

Top-of-funnel AI is a volume play. Mid-funnel AI is a conversion play.

One fills the pipe. The other turns the pipe into revenue.

Guess which one most teams haven't built yet.
posts.voiceNotes: Saturation observation opener, 'problem has nothing to do with technology' verbatim, arrow bullets parallel structure, declarative close.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: The GTM Engineer Can't Replace Your RevOps Leader
posts.postText:
**The market is selling you a fantasy: a GTM engineer plus AI replaces your RevOps leader.**

It doesn't.

I was on a call recently with a GTM engineer who, mid-conversation, asked me what ARR stood for.

Smart engineer. Talented builder. But the second a board asks why net revenue retention dropped 8 points, or why your pipeline coverage looks healthy but your win rate is collapsing — that engineer is not the person you want answering.

Here's the gap nobody is naming:

GTM engineering owns the How.
The CEO and CRO own the Why.
The VP of RevOps owns the What — the translation of business strategy into the processes, the data architecture, the measurement systems that actually scale a company from 5 reps to 100.

If you cut the What layer, you don't get a leaner org. You get a faster broken one.

AI is raising the floor for everyone. It is not replacing the person who decides what gets built and why.
posts.voiceNotes: Bold thesis hook, 'It doesn't.' fragment payoff, concrete ARR anecdote, Why/What/How framework, 'AI is raising the floor' POV close.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Open a Google Sheet: The 4-Hour Exercise That Separates AI Operators from AI Tourists
posts.postText:
**If you're a senior analyst waiting for your company to enable you on AI, you've already lost.**

The people pulling ahead right now aren't waiting for a rollout plan. They're spending 4 hours on a Saturday with a Google Sheet.

Here's the exercise:

1. Open a blank sheet. Column A: every recurring task you do (daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly).
2. Column B: level of effort (low / medium / high).
3. Column C: manual or automated today.
4. Column D: could an agent do this? (yes / no / partially)
5. Column E: if yes — what would the agent need access to?

That's it. No prompts, no tooling decisions, no platform debate.

What you end up with is a personal automation roadmap that ranks itself. The high-effort, high-frequency, fully-automatable tasks at the top are your week-one wins.

Four hours of this puts you ahead of 90% of your peers — because most people are still consuming AI content instead of mapping their own work.

At LeanScale we tell every new operator on the team to do this in their first month. The ones who do it twice in their first 90 days are the ones we end up promoting.
posts.voiceNotes: Bold contrarian hook, numbered steps for SOP feel, 'That's it.' fragment, LeanScale internal anecdote close.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Stop Talking About Top of Funnel AI — Mid Funnel Is Where the Gold Is
posts.postText:
**Every AI-in-GTM post on this platform is about top of funnel. Meanwhile the actual revenue is being unlocked in the mid funnel.**

What I'm seeing across the GTM teams we work with:

The top-of-funnel stuff — 10,000-step lead enrichment, agentic prospecting, the 47-tool workflow diagrams — is loud, demo-friendly, and mostly marginal. It moves a percentage point or two on reply rates.

The mid-funnel work is quietly eating the revenue ceiling:

— SOWs auto-generated from call transcripts in under 10 minutes
— AI deal coaching tuned to your actual playbook, not a generic framework
— GTM diagnostics that turn a 2-week scoping engagement into a 1-hour deliverable
— MEDDIC auto-populated from Gong, then triangulated against rep input to find the gaps

Why? Because the hard part of a complex sale isn't getting a lead. It's qualification, deal progression, and matching what the buyer actually needs to what you sell.

If you're a CRO and your AI roadmap is 80% prospecting agents and 20% mid-funnel — flip it.

That's where the next 10 points of win rate live.
posts.voiceNotes: Bold thesis, 'What I'm seeing across' pattern opener, dash bullets, 'Why?' pivot, sharp actionable directive close.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: We Got 10x Faster and Almost Lost Revenue
posts.postText:
**We got 10x faster at one of our core delivery workflows. Our first reaction was panic.**

Here's what happened:

A workflow that used to take our team a full day was suddenly taking 45 minutes. Great for the customer. Terrifying for the P&L — because we bill for the work.

For about a week we were genuinely concerned we were cannibalizing our own business. The better we got, the less revenue per engagement we'd generate.

Then something interesting happened.

The customer saw what we delivered, and immediately asked us to do three more things they'd been sitting on for months. Things they couldn't justify previously because the cost-to-value math didn't work.

Now the math works. So they expand.

And then we made a choice that felt counterintuitive at first — we passed some of the speed gains back to the customer as price compression on the original work. Not because we had to. Because it's the kind of move that turns a vendor into a partner.

The lesson: in a services business, AI doesn't reduce demand. It exposes all the work your customer was already deferring.

Every services firm is about to ride this curve. The ones who pass value back early win the next decade.
posts.voiceNotes: Bold first-person thesis, 'Here's what happened:' Anthony phrase, concrete numbers, services-as-software thesis close.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: The Build Order: CRO, Then RevOps, Then Enablement, Then AEs
posts.postText:
**Most founders' first GTM hire after themselves is an AE. That's why most early AEs fail.**

The build order matters more than the hires.

Swipe through for the sequence we recommend to every founder transitioning out of founder-led sales — and the specific reason each role has to come before the next.

AEs hitting a built machine ramp in weeks. AEs hitting chaos ramp in quarters. Or never.

Get the order right and you save yourself 18 months of misattributed 'we hired the wrong rep' decisions.
posts.voiceNotes: Bold contrarian hook, short caption, operator-confident close.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 4-panel carousel. Panel 1 (cover): 'The GTM Build Order' with LeanScale logo, dark mode, lime accent. Panels 2-5: one role per panel in sequence — (2) CRO: owns the Why. Sets the strategy and the number. (3) RevOps: owns the What. Builds the measurement backbone, the data architecture, the processes. (4) Enablement: owns the How for humans. Codifies the playbook so the next 10 reps don't reinvent it. (5) AEs: execute against a working system. Each panel has a one-line description, an icon, and the failure mode if skipped (e.g., 'Skip RevOps and your AEs forecast from gut feel for 18 months'). Final panel: 'Most founders go 1 then 4. The good ones go 1, 2, 3, 4.' Plus Jakarta Sans, lime/purple on dark.

params.title: The GTM Build Order.
params.body: The sequence that saves you 18 months of misattributed 'we hired the wrong rep' decisions.
Sets the strategy and the number. Skip this and the next 3 hires are guessing.
Builds the measurement backbone, the data architecture, the processes. Skip this and your AEs forecast from gut feel for 18 months.
Codifies the playbook so the next 10 reps don't reinvent it. Skip this and ramp time triples.
They drop into a working machine, ramp in weeks, and outperform anyone hired earlier in the wrong order.

params.title: Most founders go 1 then 4. The good ones go 1, 2, 3, 4.
